title: Seattle · Four Decades of Crime
subtitle: FBI UCR 1985–2016 + SPD NIBRS 2017–2026 · by neighborhood · real incident locations

copy.chapter2Title: The map comes alive — by neighborhood
copy.chapter2Caption: Live monthly reports across 58 MCPP neighborhoods. Every dot is a real reported offense location (block-level, sampled).
copy.transitionDesc: From here the data is incident-level across 58 neighborhoods — real monthly counts, and every dot is a real reported offense location, anonymized to the block.
copy.methodRecentSub: 2017–2026 · by neighborhood, month by month · real incident locations
copy.methodDotsSub: Every dot is a real reported offense location (block-level). Neighborhood detail begins 2017 — earlier records are counted, never guessed onto the map.
copy.methodFootnote: FBI UCR (through 2016) and SPD NIBRS (2017+) are different measures — shown as two chapters, not one line.
copy.sourceLine: Data: Seattle Police Department via data.seattle.gov (Public Domain) · some locations redacted by SPD — counted, not plotted
copy.coverageText: 98.4% of 2017+ records mapped to a neighborhood · earlier years counted citywide
copy.creditsSources: Sources: Seattle Police Department Crime Data (2008–present, Public Domain) via data.seattle.gov & FBI UCR (1985–2016) via the FBI Crime Data Explorer. Neighborhood boundaries: Seattle MCPP (Micro-Community Policing Plans). Some incident locations are redacted by SPD; those records are counted but never plotted.

historyNotes.text: 1987: the peak — 73,369 crimes reported in a single year.
1990: violent crime peaks at 7,780 reports — then begins a long decline.
By 2016, total reports fall to 42,845 — about 42% below the 1987 peak.

annotations.text: Every dot is a real reported offense location — anonymized to the block by SPD. Redacted locations are counted, never plotted.
May 2020 is the highest month on record — 12,536 reports, driven by a property-crime spike (10,186 vs 4,715 in April).
Downtown Commercial is Seattle's busiest area for Group A crime — yet reports here fell 45% between 2017 and 2025.
Capitol Hill runs against the citywide trend — up about 15% since 2017.
Citywide, reports fell about 12% from 2017 to 2025 (86,919 → 76,917).
